reasoning: Caeleb Dressel established the 100m butterfly long course world record (49.45) in 2021. The record remains intact and is considered quite formidable, as the fastest time posted at the 2024 Olympics was 49.90 by Kristóf Milák (nearly half a second slower). Swimming world records are almost exclusively broken at major peak summer competitions (Olympics, World Championships, or major national trials in early summer), when athletes are fully rested and tapered. In contrast, the period from January to April is typically characterized by heavy training blocks and regional or lower-stakes in-season meets. Furthermore, a 2026 preview confirms there is no major long course championship this year. Thus, the likelihood of anyone breaking the record in the January-April 2026 window is incredibly small. The slight graduation in probability from Jan 11 (0.95) to Apr 4 (0.92) reflects marginally growing theoretical risk over a longer time horizon (e.g., an unexpected phenomenal swim at springtime national championships or trials, or an unforeseen retrospective doping disqualification).
